title: Easter biscuits
subtitle: Spiced Shrewsbury-style biscuits for Easter
excerpt: Traditional Easter biscuits in the Shrewsbury style: spiced with mixed spice and cinnamon, studded with currants, and cut into rounds. Crisp at the edge and tender at the centre. Makes about twenty biscuits.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Easter biscuits in the Shrewsbury style documented as a regional English tradition, particularly associated with the West Country and Shropshire. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: spiced currant biscuits appear under the seasonal household entries for Eastertide. The use of mixed spice, cinnamon, and currants in a plain biscuit dough is the form recorded across both sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and fully cooled. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Bake in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be wrapped and refrigerated overnight before rolling.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 180°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Beating softened butter and sugar together until the mixture is pale and noticeably fluffy. The friction of the beaters or wooden spoon forces air into the butter, and the sugar crystals cut through the fat to create a network of tiny air pockets. Those pockets expand in the oven heat and produce a lighter, more tender texture than rubbing cold butter into flour.

content.text: Easter biscuits are descended from the Shrewsbury biscuit, a plain, spiced round that has been made in the English kitchen since at least the seventeenth century. The currants are stirred in rather than laid in a separate layer; the spice sits in the background rather than dominating. They are supposed to be restrained.
The creaming method here produces a slightly more tender biscuit than the rubbed-in Shrewsbury. Both are correct; this one is a little crisper at the edge and softer in the centre.

the softened {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes by hand or 2 minutes with a mixer. Beat in the {{eggs}} a little at a time. Stir in the {{lemon}} zest. Add the {{plain-flour}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}}, and fold until a dough just comes together. Stir in the {{currants}}. Wrap and chill for 20 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 5 mm thick. Cut into rounds with a 7 cm cutter and arrange on a lined baking tray. Bake for 15–18 minutes, until pale gold at the edges and set across the surface. The centres will still feel tender when pressed; they firm as the biscuits cool. Transfer to a rack.
